messages.content: In the future, we might get a 119th element. But so far, it is been almost 13 years and no new elements.

Scientists are still working on synthesizing new elements through nuclear reactions, and there are ongoing efforts to create elements beyond the current limit of 118. However, the process is extremely complex and requires advanced technology, and there is no guarantee that a 119th element will be discovered anytime soon. It could take many more years of research and experimentation before we see any new elements added to the periodic table.
Or, it could happen in the next year, 2024, or even this year, 2023.

As an AI language model, I cannot predict the future or the exact timeline for the discovery of new elements. While it is possible that new elements could be discovered in the near future, there are also a lot of unknown factors that could delay or prevent such discoveries. Ultimately, the timeline for the discovery of new elements will depend on a number of different factors, including advancements in technology, scientific breakthroughs, and collaborations among scientists.
So all we know is it could be anywhere from now (2023) to never (118 is the max possible).

Yes, that is correct. Scientists are still working to synthesize new elements beyond the current maximum of 118, but it is uncertain when or if they will succeed. Yes, how long has it been since the last element was discovered.

The last element to be officially confirmed and added to the periodic table was Tennessine, element 117, which was discovered by a collaborative team of Russian and American scientists in 2010. Its discovery was officially recognized by the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) in November 2016. Therefore, it has been over five years since the most recent element was added to the periodic table.
Well, 2010 is when the element was synthesized, so it has been about 13 years since then.

Tennessine, element 117, was first synthesized by a collaborative team of Russian and American scientists in April 2010, at the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research in Dubna, Russia. The synthesis of Tennessine was achieved by bombarding Berkelium-249 with Calcium-48 ions.

Maybe it was first officially named by some other chemistry lab.

No, the official naming of new chemical elements is done by the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC). The IUPAC is the organization that sets the standards for the naming of elements and compounds and maintains the periodic table of elements. The naming process is heavily regulated and requires that the discovery of a new element be verified and confirmed by an independent committee of experts. Once the discovery is confirmed, the IUPAC will work with the discoverers to decide on an appropriate name for the new element. Therefore, the official name of Tennessine was assigned by the IUPAC, and it was done in November 2016, after a long review process.
Other labs can still have a say. Like the IUPAC only recognized element 117 in 2016 even though most people already had element 117 on their periodic tables.

Yes, you are correct that other labs or scientific organizations may have an influence on the final naming decision made by the IUPAC. The discovery of a new element requires a collaborative effort by scientists from around the world, and it is typically recognized by many researchers and organizations before the IUPAC takes the official decision. In the case of Tennessine, while the element was first synthesized in 2010, its existence was not yet fully confirmed until 2014 when further experiments were conducted. After that, the element 117 was officially recognized and named as Tennessine in November 2016 after a long review process that involved collaboration between the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research and Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, as well as other organizations. Thus, other labs and organizations can play a significant role in the discovery and recognition of new elements by the IUPAC.
What are the Transfermium wars?

The Transfermium wars were a period of scientific rivalry during the 1960s and 1970s among several international scientific teams engaged in synthesizing heavy radioactive elements. The elements being synthesized were called transactinide elements and included elements 104 through 112. The rivalry between the scientific teams, including those in the Soviet Union, Germany, and the United States, was largely focused on who was the first to discover new elements and who could claim priority for their discovery.

The name "Transfermium Wars" was derived from the word "transfermium," which refers to elements 104 to 118 on the periodic table. These elements are all synthetic, and most have extremely short half-lives, making them highly unstable. The competition extended to the naming of the new elements, with various teams vying for the rights to name the elements after their own country, a prominent scientist, or a historical figure.

The conflict culminated in the discovery of element 106, which both Soviet and American teams claimed to have synthesized first. The IUPAC eventually credited both teams with the discovery and named the element Seaborgium after American physicist Glenn Seaborg, who had played a key role in the discovery of several transuranium elements. The Transfermium Wars represented a significant period of scientific achievement and rivalry, as well as international collaboration in scientific research.
well, only elements 104 to 109 were in the war.

You are correct, my apologies for the error. The Transfermium Wars were centered primarily on the discovery of elements 104 to 109, which are all transactinide elements. The competition was mostly among scientific research teams in the United States, Soviet Union, Germany, and Japan, each vying for priority in discovering these new elements. The synthesis and identification of the new elements required a great deal of expertise, advanced experimental techniques, and scientific collaboration. The rivalry eventually led to several discoveries in the field and contributed to the expansion of our understanding of the periodic table of elements.
Can you give a timeline of the war?

Sure, here's a brief timeline of the Transfermium Wars:

- 1956: Glenn T. Seaborg and his colleagues predict the existence of heavy elements beyond curium (element 96) in the actinide series of the periodic table.
- 1961: Scientists at the Joint Institute for Nuclear Research (JINR) in Dubna, Russia, unsuccessfully attempt to synthesize element 102.
- 1964: JINR announces its discovery of element 104, naming it Kurchatovium after Igor Kurchatov, a Soviet nuclear physicist. The announcement is met with skepticism and criticism from West German and American scientists, who claim that they have also synthesized the element independently.
- 1965: The Lawrence Radiation Laboratory, later known as the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, announces the discovery of element 101, naming it Mendelevium after Dmitri Mendeleev, the father of the periodic table.
- 1966: Scientists at JINR announce the synthesis of element 105 and name it Dubnium after the institute's location.
- 1967: Scientists at the University of California, Berkeley, announce their discovery and synthesis of element 102 and name it Nobelium.
- 1969: Dubnium and Nobelium are recognized and officially named by the IUPAC.
- 1970s: The discovery and naming of elements 106 through 109 were the most hotly contested during the Transfermium Wars between the Soviet Union and the United States.
- 1974: The teams at Berkeley and JINR claim simultaneous synthesis of element 106 and name it Seaborgium after Glenn T. Seaborg.
- 1982: The IUPAC recognizes both Berkeley and JINR for their contributions to the discovery and synthesis of element 106.
- 1994: The element 110, Darmstadtium, is discovered by a German scientific team led by Peter Armbruster and Gottfried Münzenberg.

The Transfermium Wars marked a period of intense competition and scientific rivalry among researchers in the field of nuclear physics and played a significant role in shaping our understanding of the periodic table of elements as it exists today.
1997: The IUPAC proposes names for elements 104 to 109, which are accepted, ending the war.

You are correct. In 1997, the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) officially proposed new names for elements 104 to 109, which were accepted, and this effectively ended the Transfermium Wars period. The new names were based on the proposal of a joint IUPAC-IUPAP committee, and recognized the contributions of various scientists and research groups to the discovery of the elements. The names proposed by the committee were: Rutherfordium (104), Dubnium (105), Seaborgium (106), Bohrium (107), Hassium (108), and Meitnerium (109). This resolution helped to resolve the rivalry among scientists and research groups, and established a more consistent naming convention for the newly discovered elements.
